topic: Someone's childhood imaginary friend appears in their niece's drawings
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the sheet of paper as if it were a venomous insect. The crayon lines depicted a creature with too many eyes and impossibly long, spindly limbs, perched precariously on a rainbow made of mismatched construction paper. It was undeniably him. Leo. Her childhood companion, the one who whispered secrets under the covers and shared elaborate, nonsensical adventures in the dark. A tight knot tightened in Elara’s chest, constricting her breath. She snatched the drawing, crumpling the edges until the paper was a tight, angry ball in her fist. "What in the world?" she muttered, her voice sharp, directed at the empty room. She paced three steps, then stopped abruptly, her jaw clenched so tightly she could feel the muscles jump beneath her skin. This was not whimsical art; this was an invasion.

[story 2]

"This is ridiculous," Mark said, his voice dangerously low, as he held up the sketchbook. The drawing featured a figure with exaggeratedly large, floppy ears and a mischievous grin—the exact caricature of Finn, the imaginary friend from his earliest years. Mark felt a flush creep up his neck, hot and immediate. He dropped the book onto the wooden floor with a thud that echoed in the quiet apartment. His hands balled into fists at his sides, his knuckles turning white. He took a deep, shaky breath, trying to force the sudden, hot surge of irritation down into his stomach. "Who authorized this?" he demanded of the silence, his tone laced with a brittle edge that made the air around him feel thick and charged.

[story 3]

I watched the crayon depiction of Barnaby—the silent, shadow-dwelling entity from my formative years—and a cold, hard feeling settled in my gut. It was a profound sense of being deeply unsettled. I didn't move for a long moment, just letting the silence stretch until it became unbearable. When I finally spoke, my voice was clipped, precise, devoid of any warmth. "This is an imposition," I stated, pushing the drawing away from me as if it were contaminated. My shoulders tensed, pulling inward as if bracing against an unseen impact. I ran a hand through my hair, a jerky, agitated motion, my eyes darting around the room, searching for the source of this inexplicable intrusion.
